flow.title: 📊 Flow: who arrived, who stayed
flow.summary: How many customers arrived, how many completed service, and how many gave up.

wait.title: ⏱ Wait time: where customers' time went
wait.summary: From arrival to departure — time split across waiting for a seat, waiting for food, eating, and other delays.

utilization.title: 🔥 Utilization: who's busiest
utilization.summary: How busy seats, staff, and cats were. The busiest one sets the pace for the whole café.

cat.title: 🐱 Cat interaction: who got a visit
cat.summary: Cat visit rate and average visits per customer. More cat time costs customers longer stays.

healthy.label: Healthy run
healthy.friendly: 🎉 This run went smoothly! {{arrived}} customers arrived, {{served}} completed service, and only {{abandonedPct}}% gave up. Every resource still had breathing room — the café ran comfortably.
healthy.expert: ✓ Healthy run — {{arrived}} arrived, {{served}} served ({{servedPct}}%), abandon rate {{abandonedPct}}%. Max utilization {{rho}}% ({{bottleneck}}) well below the 85% Kingman heavy-load threshold.

strained.label: Under strain
strained.friendly: ⚠️ The café is under pressure. {{arrived}} arrived, {{served}} served, but {{abandonedPct}}% gave up before getting a seat. The bottleneck is {{bottleneck}} at {{rho}}% — one more busy minute and it would crack.
strained.expert: ⚠️ Strained — {{arrived}} arrived, {{served}} served ({{servedPct}}%), abandon {{abandonedPct}}%. Bottleneck: {{bottleneck}} at {{rho}}%, approaching the 85% Kingman breakdown point where queue length blows up non-linearly.

overloaded.label: Overloaded
overloaded.friendly: 🚨 The café got hammered. {{arrived}} arrived, only {{served}} ({{servedPct}}%) completed service, and **{{abandonedPct}}% walked out without even sitting down**. {{bottleneck}} is pinned at {{rho}}% — the only way to make the café faster is to expand it.
overloaded.expert: 🚨 Overloaded — {{arrived}} arrived, only {{served}} served ({{servedPct}}%), abandon rate {{abandonedPct}}%. Critical bottleneck: {{bottleneck}} at {{rho}}% → effective throughput capped. Expanding this resource is the only lever; expanding others gives ~0 marginal improvement.

hint.friendly: Once any resource crosses 85% busy, even a small bump in arrivals makes the queue explode. That's not an exaggeration — it's what queueing theory predicts mathematically.
hint.expert: Kingman's formula: W_q ≈ (ρ / (1 − ρ)) × service variance term. As ρ → 1, queue length diverges. The 85% threshold is where the non-linearity starts to bite.

seat.friendly: Increase seat count. Adding staff or cats does nothing — customers never get to that stage because they can't even sit down.
seat.expert: Increase `seatCount`; staff or cat expansion does not help because customers never reach the service phase — the constraint is seat capacity, not service speed.

staff.friendly: Increase staff count to speed up ordering and cooking. Adding seats just makes the queue look longer — those extra customers won't be served any faster.
staff.expert: Increase `staffCount`; adding seats just extends the order queue (Little's Law: more L in the waitingFood stage). Staff throughput is the binding constraint.

cat.friendly: Cat utilization is maxed out — the cats can't keep up. Add more cats to raise the visit rate so more customers get petted.
cat.expert: Increase `catCount`; cat availability is the constraint on `catInteractionRate`. Other levers (interaction time, idle interval) only shift the rate curve, not its ceiling.

flowDiagram.inFlight: Still in shop at end of sim

queueTimeSeries.title: Queue length over time L(t)
queueTimeSeries.peakLabel: Queue peaked at {{n}} people around minute {{t}}

waitHistogram.titleWaitSeat: How long each customer waited for a seat
waitHistogram.titleWaitOrder: How long each customer waited for food
waitHistogram.hint: The average is a single point; the shape of the distribution tells the real story — a long tail means a few customers waited much longer than most.

keyMoments.title: Key moments
keyMoments.clickHint: click a bubble to jump to that instant
keyMoments.jumpHint: Want to see what the café looked like at a specific moment? Click any bubble to jump into the Playback replay right there.
keyMoments.clusterLabel: {{n}} key moments at the same instant

kingman.title: Theory vs simulation (Kingman approximation)
kingman.unstableHint: Utilization reached {{rho}}%; theory predicts queue length to grow without bound. The simulator truncated the blow-up via maxWaitTime.

verdict.close: ✓ Theory and observed wait differ by just {{delta}}% — Kingman's approximation works well in this regime
verdict.diverge: ⚠ {{delta}}% gap: Kingman's Poisson + exponential-service assumptions diverge from NekoServe's actual behaviour
verdict.fail: 🔥 {{delta}}% gap: cat visits, abandon timeouts, and truncated-normal service all break Kingman's base assumptions — this is exactly why DES is more practical than pure theory

stayDist.title: Stay time distribution: cat-visited vs not
stayDist.deltaBoth: Cat-visited average stay: {{withCat}} min; without a visit: {{withoutCat}} min — delta {{delta}} min
stayDist.onlyWithCat: Every customer met a cat this run (avg stay {{avg}} min)
stayDist.onlyWithoutCat: No customer met a cat this run (avg stay {{avg}} min)

kingman.label: Kingman's approximation
kingman.def: A simple formula for estimating queue wait time. It shows wait grows proportional to utilization divided by (1 minus utilization) — so once utilization nears 100%, wait times explode. Derived by John Kingman in 1961.

littlesLaw.label: Little's Law
littlesLaw.def: The foundational identity of queueing theory: average number in system = arrival rate × average stay time. It holds regardless of the complexity of the system or the shape of the probability distributions. Proven by John Little in 1961.

poisson.label: Poisson process
poisson.def: A mathematical model of 'random events happening over time'. On average λ per time unit, but in practice sometimes you get a burst, sometimes silence. NekoServe uses this to generate customer arrivals, which is why several customers may show up together and then nobody for a while.

utilization.label: Utilization ρ (rho)
utilization.def: The fraction of time a resource is busy, from 0% to 100%. Once ρ crosses 85%, even small bumps in arrivals can blow the queue up non-linearly — that's why real-world operators rarely run resources flat out.

lambdaRate.label: Arrival rate λ (lambda)
lambdaRate.def: Average number of customers arriving per time unit. For example λ = 0.5/min means one customer roughly every two minutes. λ is the single parameter of a Poisson arrival process.

waitTime.label: W_q (queue wait time)
waitTime.def: The time a customer spends waiting in the queue before service begins. In Little's Law it's part of W (W = wait + service). For M/M/c systems, W_q can be computed exactly via the Erlang-C formula.

queueLength.label: Queue length L(t)
queueLength.def: Number of customers in the system at time t. L(t) fluctuates over time; its time-averaged value L̄ is what Little's Law refers to.

mmc.label: M/M/c model
mmc.def: Classic queueing-theory notation: first M = Markovian (Poisson) arrivals, second M = Markovian (exponential) service times, c = number of servers. NekoServe's staff station can be approximated as M/M/c with c = staffCount.

simpy.label: SimPy and env.run
simpy.def: SimPy is Python's discrete-event simulation framework. `env.run(until=T)` advances the simulation clock to time T, handling all scheduled events along the way. Any processes still running when the clock hits T get truncated.

des.label: DES (Discrete Event Simulation)
des.def: A simulation method where the clock doesn't tick second-by-second — it jumps straight to the next scheduled event. Idle stretches are skipped, so you can simulate a full day in milliseconds.

exponential.label: Exponential distribution
exponential.def: The probability distribution of 'time between random events'. Its key property is memorylessness — how long you've been waiting doesn't change how long you'll keep waiting. Inter-arrival times in a Poisson process follow this distribution.

erlangC.label: Erlang-C formula
erlangC.def: Computes the exact probability that an arriving customer has to wait in an M/M/c system. Directly connected to W_q and is the standard tool for sizing call centers, emergency rooms, and other multi-server queues.

inFlight.label: In-flight customers
inFlight.def: Customers who hadn't finished their full journey when the simulation clock hit its upper bound. They might still be dining, waiting for staff, or being visited by a cat. SimPy's env.run forcibly truncates their process, so they count as neither served nor abandoned.

bottleneck.label: Bottleneck
bottleneck.def: The resource with the highest utilization; it caps overall system throughput. Expanding the bottleneck directly increases capacity; expanding any other resource does nothing. In NekoServe this could be seats, staff, or cats.

reneging.label: Reneging (early abandonment)
reneging.def: A customer who joined the queue but gives up before being served. Ancker & Gafarian (1963) classically assume patience time is Exponential. NekoServe's maxWaitTime is the reneging threshold. Note: different from balking, which is refusing to join in the first place.

balking.label: Balking (refusing to join)
balking.def: A customer who sees the queue, decides it's too long, and walks away without even joining. Classical assumption: P_balk = n/N (n = current queue length, N = capacity cap). In NekoServe this happens when seats are full.

logNormal.label: Log-Normal distribution
logNormal.def: A distribution whose logarithm is Normal. Widely used for service times: it naturally excludes negative values yet allows rare long tail events. Dbeis 2024 empirically fit drive-thru service times to Log-Normal (mean 2.01 min, σ=0.9 min).

weibull.label: Weibull distribution
weibull.def: A continuous distribution with a tunable shape parameter, often used for failure times or skewed interarrival intervals. Hasugian 2020 curve-fit Indonesian fast-food arrivals to Weibull (mean 67.5 s), which differs from Dbeis 2024's Poisson result — evidence that fit depends on context.

rcrf.label: RCRF (Reneging Customers Relative Frequency)
rcrf.def: A nonlinear regression proposed by Dbeis 2024 (R²=0.945): RCRF = -2.056S + 1.37·eˢ - 1.173, where S ∈ [0,1] is peak-hour sensitivity (closer to peak = bigger S). Models how reneging density scales with peak pressure.

rhoCorrected.label: ρ_R (Dbeis 2024 corrected utilization)
rhoCorrected.def: Dbeis 2024 corrected utilization: ρ_R = (λ − RR) / μ. Subtract reneging rate RR from λ before dividing by μ, since early-leavers never actually consumed server time. Avoids the 'ρ > 1 apparent collapse' mistake under heavy reneging.

normalDist.label: Normal / Gaussian distribution
normalDist.def: The classical bell curve, determined by mean and standard deviation. NekoServe uses Normal (σ = 0.2μ, floored at 1 min) for order/prep/dining times. Hasugian 2020's curve fit also landed on Normal for fast-food service times.

curveFit.label: Curve fitting
curveFit.def: The process of selecting the best-fitting distribution and parameters from observed data, using tools like EasyFit. This is what my advisor meant by 'every shop is different' — you have to curve fit a target shop's own data to get its distributions.

mm1.label: M/M/1 model
mm1.def: The simplest Kendall-notation queueing system: Poisson arrivals, Exponential service, one server. Dbeis 2024's drive-thru study is M/M/1 because the ordering window is a single staff station.

plsSem.label: PLS-SEM (Partial Least Squares SEM)
plsSem.def: A statistical method for latent-variable causal models, well-suited to small samples and nonlinear relationships. Li et al. (2025) used PLS-SEM on 423 Chinese pet-café customers and found pet interactivity, cuteness, and cleanliness dominate; coffee quality is insignificant.

chiSquare.label: Chi-square test
chiSquare.def: A statistical test of whether observed data fits an assumed distribution. Higher p-values mean better fit. Dbeis 2024 ran chi-square on all three distributions (arrivals, service, reneging) with p-values 0.414, 0.634, 0.669 — all passing, which is why they could confidently claim Poisson / Log-Normal / Exponential.

agentBased.label: Agent-based modeling
agentBased.def: Modeling each individual (customer, cat) as an autonomous agent whose interactions produce system behavior. NekoServe's cats are agents: each cat is its own SimPy process that decides when to visit customers and when to rest — not a passive queue resource.

maxWaitTime.label: maxWaitTime (patience threshold parameter)
maxWaitTime.def: How many minutes a queued customer will wait before giving up. Cumulative wait above this triggers reneging (leaving the queue). NekoServe's hard-threshold simplification of the patience distribution from Ancker & Gafarian (1963) and Dbeis (2024).

customerArrivalInterval.label: customerArrivalInterval (arrival-gap parameter)
customerArrivalInterval.def: Average inter-arrival time (minutes) used to sample customer arrival times. Actual gaps are drawn from Exp(1 / customerArrivalInterval), producing a Poisson arrival process. Justified by Hasugian et al. (2020).

ethogram.label: Ethogram (behavior catalog)
ethogram.def: Animal-behavior tool that lists every observed behavior with its time share. Hirsch 2025's 9-state ethogram compresses 227 hours of observation into percentages (resting 31.7%, socializing 12.8%, playing 0.3%, and so on). NekoServe's cat FSM reuses this distribution as its state-transition probability table.

catWelfareScore.label: CatWelfareScore (composite welfare index)
catWelfareScore.def: NekoServe's home-grown 0–5 score that blends the five welfare sub-indicators suggested by Hirsch 2025 (play / explore / maintenance = higher is better; hiding / alert = lower is better). Plotted beside customer satisfaction to make the human/cat trade-off visible.

paretoFrontier.label: Pareto frontier
paretoFrontier.def: The set of multi-objective solutions no other solution dominates on both axes simultaneously. NekoServe plots customer satisfaction vs cat welfare as a 2D scatter; green-ringed dots are on the Pareto frontier, meaning you can't improve one without giving up the other.

goodnessOfFit.label: Goodness-of-fit
goodnessOfFit.def: A family of statistical tests that check whether observed data match a theoretical probability distribution. Chi-square goodness-of-fit (χ² GoF) is the most common: compare observed counts vs expected counts; larger disparity = worse fit. Hirsch 2025 uses it to validate cat vertical-level preferences (χ²(2) = 1234.2).

ksDivergence.label: Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS)
ksDivergence.def: A non-parametric distribution comparison: the D statistic is the max vertical distance between two CDFs. Advantage: no distributional assumption. Disadvantage: insensitive to differences in small-probability categories. Critical value D₀.₀₅ ≈ 1.36/√n (Massey 1951).

klDivergence.label: Kullback-Leibler divergence (KL)
klDivergence.def: An information-theoretic distance: D_KL(P‖Q) = Σ p·log(p/q). Interpretation: how many extra nats (or bits) you pay per observation when encoding P using a code optimised for Q. Zero means identical distributions; larger means P puts probability mass on the wrong categories.

wilsonCI.label: Wilson score confidence interval
wilsonCI.def: A 95% confidence interval for a binomial proportion (like '49.2% of time on SHELF'). More stable than the naive Wald interval p̂ ± 1.96·√(p̂(1-p̂)/n): doesn't collapse at p near 0 or 1, behaves well at small n. Wilson (1927) is the original; current standard (Agresti 2013).

cdf.label: Cumulative distribution function (CDF)
cdf.def: F(x) = P(X ≤ x). Turns a point-density view of a distribution into a running-total view. Always non-decreasing, runs from 0 to 1. KS test compares two CDFs' maximum vertical gap.

pValue.label: p-value
pValue.def: The probability of observing this-much-or-more deviation if H₀ is true. p < 0.05 is the conventional 'statistically significant' threshold. Caveat: p-values amplify with n, so any small difference becomes significant in a large sample — 'p < 0.001' alone doesn't mean 'substantively important'.

criticalValue.label: Critical value
criticalValue.def: The threshold a test statistic must exceed to reach significance at a chosen α (usually 0.05). Chi-square at df = 8, α = 0.05 has critical value 15.51; observed χ² larger than that rejects H₀.

degreesOfFreedom.label: Degrees of freedom (df)
degreesOfFreedom.def: The number of truly-free parameters behind a test statistic. For a 9-category chi-square goodness-of-fit, df = 9 - 1 = 8 (subtract 1 because total probability must equal 1). Critical values vary with df.

stdResidual.label: Standardized residual
stdResidual.def: (O − E) / √E. More informative than raw residuals because it removes the scale effect (big-E categories naturally have bigger raw residuals). Rule of thumb: |std residual| ≥ 2 is worth attention; ≥ 3 is notable deviation.

residual.label: Residual
residual.def: Observed minus expected (O − E). In chi-square analysis tells you which category deviates most and in which direction. Pearson residual is a stricter-standardised variant (divides by √(1-E) on top of √E).

nats.label: nats
nats.def: The natural-log unit of information. 1 nat = log₂(e) ≈ 1.443 bits. Whether KL reports nats or bits is a convention choice; both are equivalent up to a constant factor.

monteCarlo.label: Monte Carlo
monteCarlo.def: Simulation of complex systems by drawing many random samples. Every NekoServe run is a Monte Carlo trial; running the same config with different seeds produces slightly different numbers, and that variation is Monte Carlo sampling error.

bonferroni.label: Bonferroni correction
bonferroni.def: Conservative correction for multiple comparisons: testing m hypotheses simultaneously, change each test's α to α/m, avoiding 'test 20 times and by chance 1 will look significant'. Hirsch 2025's padj = 0.013 is α/m after this adjustment.

faceValidity.label: Face validity
faceValidity.def: First-pass V&V check: 'does this simulation look like the real system it's meant to represent?' Sargent (2013) lists it as the first gate — if face validity fails, fancier statistical tests are moot. Our 80-point threshold follows Sargent's face validity ≥ 0.8 convention.

compositeScore.label: Composite score
compositeScore.def: A weighted average of complementary indicators collapsed into a single comparable number. Strength: cancels single-metric blind spots (χ² misses shape shifts; KS misses small categories). Weakness: weight selection is subjective. NekoServe uses 0.4·χ² + 0.3·KS + 0.3·KL; χ² weighted higher because it's the same method the paper uses.

softCap.label: Soft-cap
softCap.def: Linear mapping of a raw statistic to 0-100: score = 100·max(0, 1 - stat/cap). stat = 0 → 100 points; stat ≥ cap → 0 points. Smoother than a hard threshold, so 'slight deviation' and 'large deviation' produce different scores.

typeIError.label: Type I error
typeIError.def: Hypothesis-testing false positive: H₀ is actually true but gets rejected. α (significance level) controls it. We deliberately loosen our soft-cap to prevent simulator Monte Carlo noise from triggering Type I errors (false-failure reports).

outlier.label: Outlier
outlier.def: A single observation far from the rest. Single statistics get dragged by outliers (e.g. arithmetic mean); medians and composite indicators are more robust. Sargent (2013) recommends multi-indicator composites precisely to resist outlier influence.

verificationValidation.label: Verification & Validation (V&V)
verificationValidation.def: Two independent checks in simulation research. Verification: 'did we code the spec correctly' (internal correctness). Validation: 'does the spec match reality' (external validity). The validation page tests the Validation side; whether simpy runs to spec is Verification.

scanSampling.label: Scan sampling
scanSampling.def: An ethology observation method: at fixed intervals (10 minutes in Hirsch 2025), record what every animal is doing at that instant. Captures average time-shares well, misses rare bursts. Complements all-occurrence and focal sampling.

allOccurrenceSampling.label: All-occurrence sampling
allOccurrenceSampling.def: Targets a specific behavior (e.g. cat-cat interaction); every instance during the observation window is logged exhaustively, no sampling. Captures rare events that scan sampling would miss. Hirsch 2025 used it for all 349 cat-cat interactions.

focalSampling.label: Focal sampling
focalSampling.def: Picks one focal individual and continuously records all of its behaviors for a fixed time. Hirsch 2025 used 15-minute focal windows per cat per day to capture human-cat interactions in detail.

boris.label: BORIS (Behavioral Observation Research Interactive Software)
boris.def: Open-source ethology recording software (v 7.9.6 in Hirsch 2025). Lets researchers hotkey-log behaviors and durations in real time; output feeds straight into statistical analysis. Standard tool for ethogram research.

inLounge.label: In-lounge
inLounge.def: Hirsch 2025 terminology: cat is in the customer-accessible lounge (Area 1 + Area 2). The in-lounge subset (n = 8,547 = 12,505 total - 3,948 OUT_OF_LOUNGE scans) is what the vertical-level distribution is computed over.

figure3Hirsch.label: Figure 3 (Hirsch 2025)
figure3Hirsch.def: Core figure of Hirsch et al. (2025), in two panels: left shows the three-area distribution (Area 1 / Cat Room / Area 2), right shows the vertical-level breakdown on the in-lounge subset (Floor / Furniture / Shelf). NekoServe's validation page aligns simulation output against Figure 3.

figure6Hirsch.label: Figure 6 (Hirsch 2025)
figure6Hirsch.def: Crosstab of customer demographics × human-cat attention modes (Women / Men / Girls / Boys × No interaction / Non-contact / Contact / No attention from human). The paper's body text reports χ²(18) but the caption reports χ²(16) — flagged by NekoServe as an open paper inconsistency.

mannWhitneyU.label: Mann-Whitney U test
mannWhitneyU.def: Non-parametric test comparing the medians of two independent groups, no normality assumption. Hirsch 2025 used it for weekday vs weekend customer flow: U(41,26) = 212.00, z = -5.53, p < 0.001, r = 0.68 (large effect).

effectSizeR.label: Effect size r
effectSizeR.def: Derived from the Mann-Whitney z: r = z / √N. Conventional thresholds: 0.1 small / 0.3 medium / 0.5 large. Hirsch 2025's weekday-vs-weekend r = 0.68 is a large effect, well above noise.

facultativeSocial.label: Facultative sociality
facultativeSocial.def: Domestic cats' adaptive social strategy: can be solitary or live in groups depending on resource availability and environmental stability. Unlike obligately social dogs or strictly solitary tigers. The cat café's confined space tests exactly this flexibility, and explains why multi-level perches and hide-spots matter for welfare.

feignedSleep.label: Feigned sleep
feignedSleep.def: A cat appearing to sleep while remaining vigilant — a stress response. Hirsch 2025 acknowledges in Limitations that a single observer cannot distinguish feigned from genuine sleep, which may inflate the RESTING share. NekoServe does not model the distinction either.

affiliative.label: Affiliative interaction
affiliative.def: Friendly cat-cat contact such as mutual grooming, sitting close, head-bunting. Hirsch 2025 observed 53% of cat-cat events as affiliative; not significantly different from agonistic 47% (χ²(1) = 1.264, p = 0.261). NekoServe uses Bernoulli(0.53).

agonistic.label: Agonistic interaction
agonistic.def: Conflict behaviors between cats: hisses, swats, chases, threat postures. 47% of Hirsch 2025's observations. Domestic cats lack effective conflict-minimization signals, so they rely on distance — confined spaces strip them of this option. NekoServe uses Bernoulli(0.47).

occupancyLevel.label: Occupancy level
occupancyLevel.def: Hirsch 2025 bins the simultaneous customer count into three levels: LOW < 21, MID 21-29, HIGH > 29. Many cat behaviors (especially SHELF preference, ALERT, HIDDEN) shift significantly across these levels, which the paper validates with χ² tests of independence. NekoServe inherits the bins as occupancy modifiers.

flow.friendly: NekoServe uses a Poisson process to generate customer arrivals: on average λ people per minute, but in practice sometimes two arrive at once and sometimes nobody shows for a while. That's "random but statistically predictable". Customers still inside the café at the end of the simulation are called in-flight — they didn't finish because env.run(until=...) cut the clock off.
flow.friendlyFormula: arrivals = served + gave up + still in-flight at end
flow.expert: Arrivals follow a Poisson process: on average one customer every fixed interval, but actual inter-arrival times are independent exponential random variables. When the simulation clock hits its upper bound, SimPy's env.run truncates any customer processes still mid-flow — those neither served nor abandoned are the residual term in the formula below.

wait.friendly: Total time in café = waiting for a seat + waiting for food + eating + time spent frozen by a cat on your lap. Waits grow when the resource is busy. Cats physically trap the customer for the duration of each visit, which is why more cats inflate the average stay time.
wait.friendlyFormula: total stay ≈ wait seat + wait food + dining + other (incl. cat)
wait.expert: Little's Law is the time-averaged identity: the average number in the system equals the effective arrival rate times the average stay time. Under the M/M/c approximation, every wait phase scales with utilization: higher utilization means longer waits. Cat visits pin a customer in the dining stage for extra time, effectively adding a cat-delay term to the average stay.

utilization.friendly: Utilization is the percentage of time a resource is busy. 100% means no slack at all; 0% means completely idle. Higher is NOT always better — once you cross 85%, even tiny bursts of arrivals cause the queue to explode, because there's no slack to absorb the variance. The busiest resource is the bottleneck; expanding it is the only way to speed up the whole café.
utilization.friendlyFormula: utilization = actual busy time ÷ total available time
utilization.expert: For a multi-server station with c servers, utilization equals arrival rate times mean service time divided by server count. Kingman's approximation shows queue wait grows proportional to utilization divided by (1 minus utilization) — that's why crossing 85% makes queues blow up non-linearly. System throughput is capped at server count divided by mean service time, so expanding any non-bottleneck resource gives zero benefit.

cat.friendly: Cat visit rate = the share of customers visited by at least one cat. Higher is good for happiness but bad for stay time — each visit locks the customer in place. To get both high visit rate AND reasonable stay time, you need more cats (catCount) or shorter interaction time (catInteractionTime).
cat.friendlyFormula: visit rate = customers visited by a cat ÷ total served customers
cat.expert: Cats have been autonomous SimPy processes since v0.4.0, cycling through idle, visiting, and optional resting states. Each visit keeps a customer locked in the dining stage for the full interaction time, directly inflating average total stay time but leaving staff and seat service rates completely untouched. Cat utilization tracks the fraction of total simulation time spent busy (visiting plus resting).

totalCustomersArrived.label: Total arrivals
totalCustomersArrived.description: Customers who arrived at the café during the simulation

totalCustomersServed.label: Customers served
totalCustomersServed.description: Customers who completed the full service flow

abandonRate.label: Abandon rate
abandonRate.description: Share of customers who gave up after waiting for a seat

catInteractionRate.label: Visited-by-cat rate
catInteractionRate.description: Share of served customers who got at least one cat visit

avgCatVisitsPerCustomer.label: Avg. cat visits
avgCatVisitsPerCustomer.description: Average number of cat visits per served customer

avgTotalStayTime.label: Avg. total stay
avgTotalStayTime.description: Average time from arrival to departure

avgWaitForSeat.label: Avg. wait for seat
avgWaitForSeat.description: Average wait from arrival to being seated

avgWaitForOrder.label: Avg. wait for order
avgWaitForOrder.description: Average wait from order placed to food ready

seatUtilization.label: Seat utilization
seatUtilization.description: Fraction of time seats were occupied

staffUtilization.label: Staff utilization
staffUtilization.description: Fraction of time staff were busy

catUtilization.label: Cat utilization
catUtilization.description: Fraction of time cats were busy (interacting or resting)

batch.banner: Results are based on {{n}} replications (different random seeds)
batch.ciHint: 95% confidence intervals shown below each KPI

whatIf.expand: What-If Explorer: tweak parameters and compare instantly
whatIf.title: What-If Explorer
whatIf.adjustHint: Drag the sliders to adjust, results update in real time

history.title: Simulation history
history.empty: No history yet. Run a simulation first!
history.clearConfirm: Clear all history? This cannot be undone.
history.maxCompare: You can compare up to 4 runs
history.capHint: History keeps the most recent 20 runs. Each new run evicts the oldest unpinned entry; press 📍 to pin a run so it survives.

comparison.title: Comparison view
comparison.legend: Green = better · Red = worse (only marked when spreads are meaningful)

eventLog.title: Event log
eventLog.description: Full simulation event timeline, sorted by simulation time. Filter by type or search by keyword.

customerPie.title: 🐱 Customer outcome distribution
customerPie.clickHint: (click a slice to open the event log)

lambda.label: Arrival rate
lambda.desc: How many customers walk in per minute, on average. Imagine standing at the door with a clicker, counting heads. If 12 people come in an hour, the rate is 12/60 = 0.2 per minute.
lambda.example: You set 'one customer every 5 min,' so arrival rate = 1/5 = 0.2. That means 0.2 people walk in per minute on average.

invArrival.label: Inverse of interval
invArrival.desc: Just flipping the question: 'one customer every 5 minutes' becomes '0.2 customers every minute.' Like converting speed from 'hours per mile' to 'miles per hour.' It's the same info, just flipped.

Xk.label: Gap between customer k-1 and k
Xk.desc: How long between the last customer and the next one. The key point: it's different every time! Sometimes they come back-to-back, sometimes there's a long gap. Like a convenience store: you can't predict when the next person pushes the door open.
Xk.example: Customer #1 came after 3 min, #2 after 8 min, #3 after just 1 min.

expDist.label: Exponential distribution
expDist.desc: A pattern that describes random gaps. Its most important feature: no matter how long you've already waited, the chance of someone showing up next minute stays the same. Like waiting for a bus: waiting 10 minutes doesn't make the next bus come any faster.
expDist.example: You've been waiting 20 minutes with no customer. But the chance of someone arriving in the next minute is exactly the same as it was when you first started waiting.

Nt.label: How many arrived in t minutes
Nt.desc: From opening to now, how many customers have walked in total. This number keeps climbing over time.
Nt.example: Rate 0.2/min over 240 min: about 0.2 x 240 = 48 customers. But it might actually be 42 or 55, because it's random.

poisson.label: Poisson distribution
poisson.desc: A pattern that predicts 'how many things happen in a fixed time window.' Once you know the average, it tells you the realistic range. Like knowing 12 customers come per hour on average, but some hours it's 8 and others it's 15. Poisson describes exactly that kind of wobble around the average.
poisson.example: Average 12/hour, but actual numbers wobble between roughly 8 and 16, with the occasional outlier.

L.label: Average people in the shop
L.desc: Imagine snapping a photo of the shop every minute and counting heads. Add up all the counts and divide by the number of photos. That's L. It tells you 'how crowded is it usually.'
L.example: L = 6 means if you walk in at a random time, you'd see about 6 customers (some queueing, some eating).

W.label: Average time spent in the shop
W.desc: From the moment a customer pushes the door open to the moment they walk out, how long are they inside? All waiting plus all service time, everything counts.
W.example: Wait for seat: 3 min + wait for food: 10 min + eating: 15 min = 28 min total.

Wq.label: Queue waiting time
Wq.desc: The time spent just standing in line, doing nothing, waiting for your turn. From the moment you join the queue to the moment it's finally your turn.
Wq.example: You arrive and see 2 people ahead of you. You stand there for 3 minutes before getting a seat. Those 3 minutes = Wq.

mu.label: Service speed
mu.desc: How many customers one staff member can handle per minute. Simple math: if serving one person takes 8 minutes, speed = 1/8. Like walking speed, but for processing customers.
mu.example: Making one meal takes 8 min on average. Service speed = 1/8 = 0.125 per min.

cServer.label: Number of service points
cServer.desc: How many 'windows' can serve customers at the same time. 10 seats = c is 10, 2 staff = c is 2. More windows means more customers handled simultaneously, shorter queues.
cServer.example: A bank with 3 tellers: c = 3, can serve 3 customers at once.

rho.label: How busy (utilization)
rho.desc: How busy this resource is. 0% = dead quiet. 100% = non-stop busy. Once it passes 85%, queues suddenly get very long, like highway traffic: traffic flow hits a critical point and everything jams up at once.
rho.example: 75% = busy but manageable. 90% = queues are getting long. 99% = total meltdown.

erlangC.label: Erlang-C formula
erlangC.desc: A classic formula that calculates 'how long will customers wait.' Just plug in arrival speed, service speed, and number of windows, and it predicts the wait time. Call centers use it to decide how many agents to hire.
erlangC.example: Arrival speed = 0.2/min, service time = 8 min, windows = 2. Plug these in and get the average wait.

ES.label: Average service time
ES.desc: How long it takes to serve one customer on average. E[S]: the E just means 'average' (expected value), S means 'service.' So E[S] = average service time. That's literally it.
ES.example: E[S] = 8 just means 'one service takes 8 minutes on average.' Sometimes 6, sometimes 10, but 8 on average.

Ca2.label: How chaotic are the arrivals
Ca2.desc: Measures how 'messy' customer arrivals are. = 1 means normal random (like raindrops hitting a window, no pattern but natural). > 1 means chaotic (a tour group shows up, then nothing for ages, then another group). < 1 means very regular (like appointments, evenly spaced).
Ca2.example: Ca = 1: normal random. Ca = 2: very irregular, people come in clusters. Ca = 0.5: almost like scheduled appointments.

Cs2.label: How unpredictable is service time
Cs2.desc: Some customers are quick, others take forever. This measures how big the difference is. Bigger number = some super fast, some super slow, making the queue harder to predict and waits longer.
Cs2.example: Small: every customer takes about the same time (like self-checkout). Large: some take 3 min, others 15 min (like a doctor's visit).

kingman.label: Kingman quick estimate
kingman.desc: A formula for quickly estimating wait time. Core idea: busier + more chaotic = longer waits. There's a key piece: the denominator is '1 minus utilization,' so as utilization approaches 100%, the denominator approaches 0, and wait time explodes. That's why you can't run resources at full capacity.
kingman.example: Imagine a sink almost full (95% utilization). One more drop of water (one more customer) and it overflows. That's what Kingman's formula is saying.

visitRateEff.label: Actual cat visit speed
visitRateEff.desc: How many cat visits can actually happen per minute. It's not just 'how many cats there are,' because each cat needs time to wander, interact, and possibly nap. This number accounts for all that time overhead.
visitRateEff.example: 3 cats, but each needs 4 min wandering + 10 min interaction + 30% chance of a 15-min nap. Actual visit rate is much lower than you'd think.

catCount.label: Number of cats
catCount.desc: How many cats are in the cafe. More cats = more chances for customers to get a visit. But each cat needs its own wandering and napping time, so doubling cats doesn't double the visit rate.

idleInterval.label: Cat wandering time
idleInterval.desc: After finishing a visit, the cat wanders around the cafe for a while before approaching the next customer. This 'looking for someone' time is the idle interval.
idleInterval.example: Set to 4 min means the cat wanders for about 4 minutes before walking up to the next customer.

pRest.label: Nap chance
pRest.desc: After each interaction, how likely the cat is to take a nap. Like flipping a coin: 0.3 means 30% chance of resting (roughly 1 out of every 3 interactions).
pRest.example: 0.3 = about 3 naps per 10 interactions. 0 = never rests (iron cat). 1 = always rests (lazy cat).

restTime.label: Nap duration
restTime.desc: Once a cat decides to rest, how long it sleeps on average. During the nap, it's completely unavailable, like the shop temporarily has one fewer cat.
restTime.example: Set to 15 min: the cat sleeps about 15 minutes before waking up and finding the next customer.

welfare.title: Cat Welfare Score
welfare.citation: Based on Hirsch et al. (2025), DOI: 10.3390/ani15223233
welfare.intro: Five behavioral indicators summed into a 0–5 welfare score. Positive indicators should be high; negative indicators should be low. Scenarios scoring below 2 flag as cat-unfriendly.
welfare.shelfHint: Hirsch 2025 Figure 4b: more customers → cats flee to shelves. A shelf-heavy bar here means the scenario is stressing cats.

passive.title: Passive Exposure: seeing a cat counts too
passive.tagline: Hirsch 2025 only measured contact — this is my second channel
passive.intro: Even if a cat never jumps on you, watching one stroll past, lounge on a shelf, or play with someone nearby still feels good. I accumulate that "cat-visible" time as a weighted score, reported independently so the contact-based customerSatisfactionScore stays untouched. Formula: cat and customer in the same AREA, weighted by inverse distance, with bonuses for shelf/playing cats.

kpi.ratioHint: Active contact avg {{active}} min, passive avg {{passive}} min
kpi.scoreHint: 1 − exp(−avg/15), so it aligns with other 0–1 metrics

passive.caveat: ⚠️ This metric is a hypothesis-driven model extension: Hirsch 2025 didn't measure passive exposure. Future empirical work (customer survey + regression) can validate the active/passive weighting.

passiveSweep.title: Passive-channel sensitivity: catCount sweep
passiveSweep.tagline: how does each channel scale when you add more cats?
passiveSweep.intro: Runs eight background simulations (catCount = 1..8), keeping everything else identical to the current scenario, then plots the two cat-time channels against catCount. The active curve saturates once cats are visit-bound; the passive curve keeps climbing with ambient visibility. The crossover tells you the catCount at which the ambient channel starts dominating the measured one.
passiveSweep.readingWithCrossover: Passive exposure overtakes active contact at about catCount ≈ {{catCount}} ({{value}} min). Below that threshold the measured channel dominates; above it, ambient visibility is the bigger share of the experience.
passiveSweep.readingNoCrossover: The two channels don't cross within 1..8 cats in this scenario. Either the active channel stays ahead (low-occupancy, cats always find visitors) or the passive channel is already dominant at catCount = 1 (high-occupancy, cats rarely free for contact).
passiveSweep.caveat: ⚠️ This sweep runs single-replication simulations for speed. Marginal differences under ~0.5 min/customer are noisy; re-run to see whether the pattern is stable.

pareto.title: Customer satisfaction vs Cat welfare (Pareto frontier)
pareto.intro: Each dot is one historical run. Top-right is ideal (happy customers AND happy cats). Green-ringed dots are Pareto-dominant: nothing else beats them on both axes at once.

rhoCorrection.title: Classical ρ vs Dbeis 2024 corrected ρ_R
rhoCorrection.summary: The three time-based utilizations above stay as the engine's measured truth. Here I put classical M/M/c ρ next to Dbeis & Al-Sahili's corrected ρ_R so you can line it up with the literature.
rhoCorrection.classicalHint: Arrival rate ÷ (staff × per-server service rate). Values near 1 mean staff are slammed.
rhoCorrection.correctedHint: Strip out customers who walked away before being served — that's what staff actually carry.
rhoCorrection.friendlyExplain: The idea: some customers gave up and left before being served, so they never really tied up staff time. Strip them out first, and ρ tells you whether the system is actually overloaded.
rhoCorrection.warningTitle: ⚠ Classical formula reads as a collapse
rhoCorrection.warningText: Classical ρ > 1 looks like the system is crashing, but after subtracting customers who walked away early, ρ_R sits below 1, meaning the system is actually coping. This is exactly the mismatch Dbeis 2024 flagged.
rhoCorrection.citationText: Formula source: Dbeis & Al-Sahili (2024), Enhancing Queuing Theory Realism, J. Management Analytics 11(4):659–674
